Acknowledgements
Joeri Timmerman would like to specifically thank Mariana Vassileva for putting me up to writing a
book, although not this one in particular. Then there’s my old gaming buddies Edwin van de Wal,
Colin Haddon and Yuri Karman, whose input and feedback in the old days provided me with a solid
```
knowledge base and a liking for GMing. Also my new gaming groups; Hayo Timmerman, Ronald
```
Oude Nijhuis, Hans de Groot, Daniel Sievers, Bram Derksen, Erik van Brug, Jeffrey Potman, Arnold
Visser, and Maik van der Meer, Marco Drop, Erik Schimmel. They have been providing me with
hours of fun and a sounding board for my ideas. Also, I wanted to thank Aaron Smalley as he has
been testing creatures for me in the early stages and providing both feedback and motivation to go
on. Dan Henry and Matt Hanson for challenging me to be my best and Nicholas Caldwell for some-
how giving me the opportunity. Lastly I want to thank my supportive girlfriend Katja Fisscher and
```
dedicate this book to my lost family and most of all; Ronald Oude Nijhuis for making game nights
```
epic for past 27 years!
Aaron Smalley would like to thank Joeri for starting the process of implementing a more balanced
and customizable mechanism for creature creation and Nicholas Caldwell for bringing me in to assist
Joeri in his efforts to bring these two books to market. I would also like to thank the many play-tes-
ters who have helped me to test out these mechanics and creatures over the years, including multiple
```
playtest groups and one-shot sessions that I have run RMU in Michigan’s tri-cities area (and three
```
```
years at Cabin Con), at GenCon (2013-2024), GaryCon (2019), as well as numerous email and PbP
```
games over the years. The sheer number of players is too many to list as it is in the hundreds, with
```
scores of sessions (approaching 100) at the conventions mentioned and well over 100 sessions in
```
and around the Tri-cities area. I w ould also like to thank the ERA/VTT folks who provided a lot of
feedback and editing assistance as they were pulling creature data into their respective systems and
```
play-testing those creatures with their test-groups; they include Maximiliano “Voriig Kye” Tabacman
```
```
(ERA Developer/Programmer), Brett “Ratcanthropy” Nash (RMU Roll20 Developer/Programmer),
```
```
and Bob “Reading backwards to stay awake” Morris (RMU Foundry Developer/Programmer) for
```
```
editing assistance, and “Dakadin” (RMU Fantasy Grounds Developer/Programmer) for help with
```
the Excel spreadsheet that caused hundreds of headaches and Nick “the Layout guy” Morawitz for
also helping with Excel issues and data-pulls and a whole slew of other assistance and encourage-
ment. And of course, Monte “the Dwabbit” Iafrate for all his help from a biology/RM standpoint,
not to mention Pete Fenlon for coming up with the “Dwabbit” name to explain Monte, after the story
```
(one of hundreds) about Monte’s hobbit halfling mother and dwarven father (it’s all in good fun,
```
```
until someone takes an arrow to the eye). And Monte's daughter, Isabella "I can fix this" Iafrate, for
```
editing help with filler/story text. I would especially like to thank my wife Rebecca for her patience,
support and understanding of my RPGing addiction/hobby.
Everyone at Iron Crown Enterprises would like to thank Brett Nash, Bob Morris,
Maximiliano Tabacman and "Dakadin" for their hard work and dedication in helping to make
Rolemaster available on virtual tabletops and supporting software, and for their rigorous playtesting
using these tools.

ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED
Chapter 1:Chapter 1:
IntroductionIntroduction
8
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED
The Rolemaster system provides players with an
exciting and varied way of creating characters of all
walks of life and from cultures and races real and
```
imagined. Now the Gamemaster (GM) has these tools
```
```
for Creatures. In RMU Creature Law I (CrL I) and
```
```
RMU Creature Law II (CrL II), creatures are made
```
essentially from certain building blocks and the nec-
essary math is precalculated. Each creature now has
the potential to become as individual as a Player Char-
acter, ready to be used as either minions employed by
the campaign villain or as the important, one-off, Non
Player Character the players will get their information
from or have the battle of their lives. Creature Law II
will allow GMs to modify any and all creatures to suit
any taste, campaign style and Power Level.
Creature Law I will expand the ways that a creature
is detailed in such areas as Skills, RRs and Movement
modes and other talents. Each Creature’s scores are
based on its Level and Archetype. An archetype is
```
like an Rolemaster Core Law (RMCL) profession. The
```
```
archetype basically diverts Development Points (DP)
```
to predetermined skills, making it possible to create
more diverse Creature builds, making Offensive Ar-
chetypes good at performing attacks and bad at learn-
ing and casting spells and Spellcaster Archetypes bad
at learning martial skills and good at learning magical
skills and spells. Archetypes simplify upgrading crea-
tures by providing precalculated paths of development
for each level of basically a neutral race: the Race then
becomes an add-on bonus for Stats and Skills while
reserving DP for purchasing needed and interesting
Talents.
Creature Law I also implements a new way of orga-
nizing the Creatures, based on their Category, Type
and Variety, making finding a specific creature much
easier and faster. This also ties in strongly with crea-
ture talents and descriptions and many creatures have
been rearranged according to their abilities.
Creature Law I provides an array of base creatures
built on the same principles as Player Characters and
makes them on par with each other, making them bal-
anced. Keeping a tight rein on the Power Level will
avoid tipping the scales to either side. Creature Law
II will add additional creatures and add details that
will allow a GM to customize creatures from both
books in a wide range of ways, including increasing
or decreasing levels, modifying their Archetypes and
further customization that will allow for the creation
of completely unique versions of creatures.
1.1. R olemasteR
Rolemaster is a revision of Iron Crown Enterprise’s
original Rolemaster game system published in 1982.
Rolemaster was originally designed to be used as a
```
complete fantasy role-playing (FRP) system or as in-
```
dividual modular components. This new version is no
different, providing a modular and easy to modify core
system that can be built upon to create a detailed RPG
rule set for any setting. There are five core books:
```
Rolemaster Core Law (RMCL) contains all the core
```
rules in three parts. Character Law contains a set of
rules for creating rich and detailed characters with a
complete and comprehensive skill system, as well as
optional talents that can be used to enrich characters
further. Arms Law contains rules for tactical combat
amongst individuals and small groups using a detailed
simulation of weapons and armor. Its combat system
covers the mechanics of attacks from weapons and
animals to elemental attacks. Colorful critical and
fumble results make every combat interesting and
memorable. Gamemaster Law offers helpful guidance
to Gamemasters, as well as rules and guidelines for
adding setting rules such as creating races, cultures,
and professions. Poison, Disease, and other hazards
that may come up in play are also covered.
```
Creature Law ( CrL) contains stats for hundreds of
```
animals, monsters, and fantastical creatures. Creature
Law will be released in two volumes. As fantasy set-
tings are often home to a variety of beasts, Creature
Law II contains rules for creating new monsters for
new and unusual settings.
```
Spell Law (SL) is a magic system with thousands of
```
spells organized into three realms of magic. This pro-
vides characters with a wide variety of spell-using op-
tions for all types of professions. Spells are organized
into lists, which are related in theme.
```
Treasure Law (TL) is a book of treasure, equipment
```
and commerce, an important part of any RPG. Rules
for crafting mundane objects as well as magical items
are included along with the Alchemist professions.
1. Introduction1. Introduction
9
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED
In some cases, the roll is only open-ended at the high
```
end (rolls of 96-00), but results at the low end are not
```
open-ended as some other mechanic is used for low
rolls. The most common case is attack rolls, where low
unmodified rolls indicate a fumble.
```
UNMODIFIED ROLLS (UM)
```
Certain results on some rolls indicate an immediate
effect and no modifications are considered. These rolls
are marked with a “UM”, such as “01-04 UM”. For ex-
ample, all weapon attacks result in a fumble if the initial
unmodified d100OE roll falls within the fumble range
of the weapon. If a roll falls into the UM range, then
no modifiers are applied to the roll and the unmodified
```
result is applied (such as a fumble). When resolving
```
```
absolute maneuvers (see Maneuvers, below), an un-
```
modified roll of 66 results in an unusual event. Success
or failure is resolved normally with all modifications,
but the outcome will be unusual in some way or have
unexpected side effects.
Optional Rules
In these rules, you will see rules separated out in
boxed text, like the box this text is in. Boxed rules
are optional or advanced rules, you can ignore them
if you choose and the rules will work fine as is. These
rules apply or do not apply at the GM’s discretion, so
if you are a player ask the GM if any particular boxed
text rule is being used.
DIE ROLLS
```
1–10 (1d10): Roll one ten-sided die to obtain a result
```
between 1 and 10. If the dice are numbered 0-9 rather
than 1-10, treat results of 0 as 10. Occasionally other
```
types of dice will be referenced (e.g., 1d6 for a six-sided
```
```
die). If the appropriate type of die is not available, roll a
```
ten-sided die and reroll any result outside the specified
```
range (e.g., for a 1d6, reroll any result above 6).
```
```
2–20 (2d10): Roll two ten-sided dice and add the two
```
results to obtain a result between 2 and 20. Initiative
uses this type of roll.
```
1–100 (d100): Rolemaster primarily uses two 10-sided
```
dice to get a result between 1 and 100, referred to as
‘percentiles’. Each of the 10-sided dice gives a result
between 0 and 9 — one die is treated as the “tens” die
and the other as the “ones” die. These may be distin-
```
guished by color or other marking (in which case it
```
must be made clear which die is to be read as the tens
```
before rolling; a fixed convention for your gaming table
```
```
is recommended), or one may use a pair of percentile
```
```
dice where one is marked “10, 20, 30, ... , 00". (It is
```
also possible to roll a novelty hundred-sided die, but
```
most gamers find these impractical.) Thus a random
```
```
result between 01 and 100 (a “00" is treated as 100) is
```
obtained. d100 rolls are used primarily for rolls on the
critical and fumble tables.
```
1–100 Open-ended (d100OE): An open-ended roll
```
means that there is no absolute minimum or maximum.
```
First make a 1-100 roll; a roll of 96-00 indicates that
```
the dice are rolled again and the result added to the first
roll. If the second roll is 96-00, then a third roll is made
and added, and so on until a non 96-00 roll is made. The
total sum of these rolls is the result of the open-ended
roll.
If the first roll is a 01-05 the dice are rolled again and
the result subtracted from the first roll. If the second roll
is 96-00, then a third roll is made and subtracted, and so
on until a non 96-00 roll is made. The total of these rolls
is the result of the low open-ended roll. A roll of 01-05
on anything other than the first roll does not cause one
to roll again.
```
Example: The GM asks a player to make an
```
```
open-ended roll, and the initial roll is a 99 (thus
```
```
between 96 and 100). A second roll is made with a
```
result of 96, so a third roll is made with a result of
04. Thus, the open-ended roll that the GM requested
```
is a 199 (= 99 + 96 + 04).
```
```
Example: The GM asks a player to make an
```
```
open-ended roll, and the initial roll is a 04 (thus
```
```
between 01 and 05). A second roll is made with a
```
```
result of 97 (thus between 96 and 00), so a third roll
```
is made, resulting in a 03. Thus, the open-ended roll
```
that the GM requested is a -96 (= 04 - 97 - 03).
```
10
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED
for their ability in the same currency and giving them
the same amount to spend at each level, allows Char-
acters to be compared one on one with the creatures.
This will benefit any GMing style out there. Creature
Law II will provide additional details as to how these
creatures can be customized by GMs as well as how
GMs can create their own custom creatures using this
same balancing/creation mechanism.
```
Stats: In Rolemaster, the stat bonuses vary from Crea-
```
ture to Creature, but based on the Archetype we can
assume some basics. The Archetype needs specific
stats to be at a specific quality for the Archetype to
be effective in their field. The Stat tables, including
emulation of how they would change over Levels,
appear in the Archetype table. For simplicity’s sake,
Creature Law uses the resulting bonus and any racial
or individual deviation may modify this.
```
Talents: Every Creature is blessed to some extent
```
with talents to improve its survival. Talents represent
abilities beyond the human norm. They are very di-
verse, ranging from Prodigy II [+10 Stalking] for a
bonus to Stalking to an Elemental cone IV [Lightning,
Lightning ball, 5', 1'D/ lvl, 1/ 3 rnds, 50x/ day] breath
weapon. Depending on how powerful the Talent is, it
costs a certain amount of DP. More details will be pro-
vided on modifying creatures in this and other ways in
Creature Law II.
```
Flaws: Every Creature has some vulnerabilities or
```
areas where it is not as strong. Some creatures might
be Uncoordinated I [+1 category penalties for current
pace] or suffer extra damage from elements via Ele-
mental Susceptibility [Heat, +1 Size]. Flaws can be
added and the creature could be stronger in other areas
because of the DP gained in acquiring the flaw. More
details will be provided on modifying creatures in this
and other ways in Creature Law II.
Please note that while there is a list of Talents and
Flaws provided at the end of each Creature’s descrip-
tion, these do not include Talents and Flaws that are
```
part of the base creature’s starting stats and concept; in
```
other words, those that modify starting Hits, Fatigue,
Base Stats, modifiers to BMR and RR’s, Size adjust-
```
ments to the base creature (note however that Talents
```
and Flaws that modify the combat mechanics away
from the creatures base size designation, such as Light
```
Boned or Dense are included). Instead, we have only
```
included those that are relevant to the more visible and
variable portion of the mechanics.
POWER LEVEL
Rolemaster is a very open role-playing game, and is
built that way on purpose to allow for a wide variety
of gaming experiences to be possible within the rules.
Power level can vary widely between two gaming
groups, or even between two different campaigns run
by the same GM. Rule elements may be over-powered
and game breaking in one campaign, but necessary or
even casual elements in another campaign. In order to
allow for a broad range of power levels, power levels
are used in some sections of the rules to identify more
powerful elements, to help GMs decide what they
want to allow in play. The GM may even decide to use
different power levels in different parts of the rules,
such as using a Heroic power level for generating
characters but an Average power level when dealing
with magic.
In Creature Law, creatures are created at the Average
power level, but may be adjusted to higher power lev-
els relatively easily. See Section 2.3 for more details.
1.2. the B asics
```
Action Point (AP): Each talent will have its own in-
```
dication for how much activity it uses up. This will
be defined as the type of action being performed, to
match the AP costs in RMCL.
Archetype is the basis for the development of a mon-
ster’s skills, and it affects everything: skill totals, OB,
DB, Fatigue, etc.
Creature Category: Creature base race: giving some
core scores, such as Base Hits, life span, extra DP,
Healing Rate, RR, perhaps even some base Stat bo-
nuses. Examples: Animals, Races, Undead.
Creature Type: A subdivision of the Creature Catego-
ry, a set of add-on abilities, to be bought by a creature
using DP to narrow down its race. Examples: Carni-
vores, Greater giant, Vampire.
Creature Variety: Subcategory of the Creature Type.
It narrows down the exact species and will indicate the
last talents and flaws to be developed, before a crea-
ture can be classified as an adult. After that they don’t
have to buy abilities any more, but still can of course.
```
Examples: Bears, Stone Giant, Sovereign vampire.
```
```
Development Points (DP): 60 DPs are awarded each
```
Level to any and all Creatures. All Creatures also re-
ceive 50 DP to buy their culture and 50 DP to buy their
racial talents and abilities. Higher Level Creatures will
have spent more DP and logically have an advantage
over lower Levels. It does not really matter how the DP
have been spent, on Skills, Talents, or other abilities,
just that they have been spent. Having Creatures pay11
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Creature LawChapter 2:Chapter 2:
CreaturesCreatures
12
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Creature Law
2. Creatures2. Creatures
2.1. classification
Creature Law assigns each Creature a classification
based on its basic abilities and its more advanced tal-
```
ents. The classification is made up of 3 parts; Creature
```
Category, Creature Type, and Creature Variety. View
this classification as a multi-part race and the creature
will fit right in the RMCL system. The classification
also allows GMs to easily find the creature they are
looking for.
CREATURE CATEGORY
First, there is the creature category, an overarching
creature type that begins to define that creature. Typ-
ical creature categories are animals and undead. The
creature’s Category binds together all those creatures
with certain overarching qualities and starts with as-
signing several qualities, such as Animals should get
a way of reproduction and Undead should be Lifeless.
This is useful in making new creatures, because tal-
ents and abilities tend to define a creature. It also aids
in looking up certain creatures, or building adventures
around certain areas. Graveyards tend to have more
undead and strange worlds may be populated by de-
mons.
The categories begin to set a basis for designing crea-
tures and acts like a determination system, used in
modern biology. By specifying more exactly which
creature you are modifying, the more precisely its
talents will be known. This is not an exact science,
however, and GMs may need to change some talents
to suit their needs.
The categories sometimes actually overlap when it
concerns, for example, an undead animal. Both the
category abilities seem to stack. Some don’t make
sense, such as a zombified horse with an herbivorous
digestive tract, so these can be ignored, but for the un-
trained eye, the horse looks like a normal horse, until
it is approached and it suddenly turns around to begin
to shamble for your brains. Category abilities are very
broad in scope and allow for a lot of tweaking, and
only give an indication of what is needed for all crea-
tures in the category.
Each category has the basic abilities, common for all
those creatures that fall into it and start to define a
common theme in a group of creatures. Giants, cen-
taurs, and humans all fall in the Race category, while
all normal animals, including dinosaurs and giant nor-
mal fauna, are also in one category together.
CREATURE TYPE
Creature type is more specialized than creature cate-
gory, more narrowly defining the creature within its
category. It represents talents as they will be used by
the creature instead of indicating broad strokes. The
Type will be adding specific ones that are needed to
fill out the creature and should be kept in mind when
designing the variety, because the variety will like-
ly dictate the tiers of the talents and the direction a
creature grows. Animals are typed according to where
they reside, most of the time, such as land, sea, and
air animals. They receive such type of abilities as they
```
need, such as mode of locomotion (hooves, flippers,
```
```
or wings) and specialized survival tools and weapons.
```
These include things like heightened senses, such as a
dog’s nose, or python’s infrared sensor pits. Creature
types narrow down the category abilities, in scope, but
might also add completely new ones that more closely
define the creatures within.
13
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Creature Law
CREATURE VARIETY
Creature Variety is a sub category of the Creature
Type and is the final point in determining the specific
creature. Varieties usually are specifications of power
within a group, adding Levels and perhaps talents to
the Creature Type. Sometimes they are subdivisions in
a Creature Type that are completely different species.
Mostly the variety indicates the final few abilities that
are added and changed from the broader specifica-
tions, between certain groups in the Creature Type.
For example, Lycanthropes come in several shapes
and sizes, classified according to their base animal
and in some cases whether it is a lesser or greater vari-
ety among their species. Lesser varieties tend to have
fewer invulnerabilities and fewer stat changes and
less chance to affect someone with the disease, while
the greater Variety is even allowed to choose whether
to inflict the disease at all. Demons can be from the
Void or the Pales, or other outer-worldly places, alto-
gether, but then diversify in many different Varieties
according to their plane of origin, or the origin of their
souls. Creature variety is the last stage in developing
a creature’s basic abilities and may define tiers that
the creature has in the various talents ascribed to it
and add things like feats, stat bonuses, racial hits and
resistances. Each variety will have slightly different
abilities, defining the creature precisely.
```
Examples: Animals are a category, including Carni-
```
vores as a type and Bears as a specific variety. The
category Undead includes Vampires as a type, with
Sovereign Vampire as a variety. The category of
Races includes Greater Giants as a type, with Stone
Giants as a variety.
2.2. aRchetypes
An archetype is a profession equivalence from the
RMCL that has been developed from level 0 up to
level 100, specifically for creatures. The archetypes
are taken from popular culture, based on Rolemaster’s
professions. Each archetype has several well-devel-
oped skills, plus some lesser skills, and each arche-
type is rated according to combat statistics and spell
use. They are further detailed and explained within
Creature Law II, for those who have an interest in
creating their own creatures or modifying existing
creatures. The basic idea is that given a level, the GM
may look in the tables and read off the archetype’s
base OB, DB, skills and spells for that level as the
basis for creating his or her own monsters. This can
be done by modifying the existing ones by level, Ar-
chetype or in any other way they may see fit while still
utilizing the same DP structure for balancing purposes
that is used in RMCL. These values are the basis for
other modifications due to special abilities, talents
and/or special and item bonuses and leave room for
a lot of variation. The Archetypes specify Stats, Skills
and other essential statistics according to the standard
rules. For every Archetype there is also a spells and PP
development category, but it does not need to be used
by every Character and Creature.
The Archetypes are more or less a framework and can
be used to come up with monsters on the fly. More
details regarding Archetypes, including the tables de-
tailing the above-mentioned values will be available
within Creature Law II as well as details on how to
modify existing creatures and guidelines for GMs to
build their own creatures that will be level-balanced
utilizing those Archetype Tables.
14
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Creature Law
2.3. poweR level
All creatures in Creature Law have been created us-
ing the same rules as player characters, except with
regards to Talent choices. An important issue in char-
acter development is the Power Level of the campaign
```
(see RMCL section 12.1). Player characters made
```
using a Power Level higher than the one the creatures
are made with will have an easier time during combat.
To increase the difficulty of encounters, the GM may
choose to use more creatures, higher-level creatures,
```
strategic advantages for the creatures (e.g., surprise or
```
```
a defensible position), or increase the power level of
```
the creatures.
Increasing the power level of a creature indicates it
is an exceptional member of its species. Just as with
```
characters, some individual creatures (or entire tribes,
```
```
packs, or breeds of creatures) stand out as unusual
```
among their kin. They may even develop reputations
and become the subject of legends. Full treatment of
power level will involve shifts to stats and skill bonus-
es which will be addressed in more detail in Creature
Law II. However, if the GM wishes to go ahead and
modify creatures to a different power level for his or
her campaign, Table 2.3 provides a quick outline of
recommendations.
```
Example: Tantrim is not a basic minotaur. The GM
```
determines he needs to be on par with the Heroic
Power Level the PCs are made with. Tantrim uses
an Offensive Archetype and GM wants this crea-
ture to hit really hard, so he applies the two +10 to
Tantrim’s main weapon skill and to his Ram attack.
The GM also decides that the +4 stat increases
will go to Strength and Constitution and the +2 go
to Agility and Quickness. This increases Tantrim’s
```
Melee OBs with +10 (St/St/Ag), his missile OBs
```
```
with +8 (Ag/Ag/St), Total Concussion Hits with +8
```
```
(Co/Co/SD) and DB with +6 (Qu/Qu/Qu). The GM
```
may record other skill increases as he sees fit. This
shows that the primary OB is 20 higher on Power
Level Heroic, from Average with Hits 8 higher and
DB increasing by +6. This is true for most creatures
of the Offensive archetype.
2.4. stats
Behind the stats block are the basic stats. In Rolemas-
ter, 10 stats define in deep detail the basic physical and
mental abilities of a Creature. Creature Law will deal
with all the detail of a Player Character, and will use
those 10 stats to the fullest extent. Instead of working
out the statistics following the rules for players, the
relevant Archetype table has the relevant stat values
for the given level, for the archetype, for an average
Creature. See RMCL section 2.5 for more information
regarding Stats.
Table 2.3 - Power Levels
Power
level
Prime
stats
Secondary
stats
Additional
modifier on skills
Resultant modifiers from Power Level to
Offensively Oriented Archetypes
Average +0 +0 +0 on skills Nothing
Superior +2 +1 +5 on 2 prime skills +10 OB, +4 Hits, +3 DB
Heroic +4 +2 +10 on 2 prime skills +20 OB, +8 Hits, +6 DB
Legendary +6 +3 +15 on 2 prime skills +30 OB, +12 Hits, +9 DB
Epic +7 +3 +20 on 2 prime skills +37 OB, +14 Hits, +9 DB
15
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Creature Law
```
Stats in Rolemaster are not static; values can and will
```
change slowly over time by gaining levels, suffering
damage, or other causes. Choice, happenstance and
strategic need guide player characters and non-player
characters on how to divide their level stat gains. In
Creature Law, these changes to stats are part of the ar-
chetype. The Offensive archetype emphasizes Strength
and Agility, while the Spell User archetype favors the
realm stat, and so forth for the other archetypes, with
stat gain rolls assumed to progress at an average rate.
Layered on top of the archetype, each creature type
may incorporate superior or inferior stat bonuses, just
as races, incurring a DP cost. The stat bonuses shown
for each creature reflect this combination of stat bonus
derived from the archetype and the creature’s race.
2.5. skills
Skills are central to the Rolemaster system. They rep-
resent the abilities that can be developed by putting
in effort and time. People and Creatures have access
to exactly the same Skills. The Archetype system re-
moves any DP calculations from the process, but the
calculations for the Skill totals need to be done. The
bonus from the applicable Stats needs to be figured
in. The Archetypes have standardized scores pre-cal-
culated for the various basic Skills listed below. With
this new Unified version of Rolemaster, the skills that
each creature possesses have already been calculated
for the GM using the new Archetype system. More
detail will be provided on how these were calculated
and how they are selected and can be modified within
Creature Law II.
```
OFFENSIVE BONUS (OB)
```
The most important ability for most creatures is their
Offensive Bonus. This is a skill the creature may de-
velop at 2 distinct rates. These are Primary OB, based
on their Archetype, for the weapons considered most
important and the Secondary OB, for the weapons
considered less important, or harder to develop.
```
DEFENSIVE BONUS (DB)
```
This represents most of the defensive bonus for a Crea-
ture. Certain Creatures, with the Defensive Archetype
or the Discipline Archetype, gain a bonus from Body
```
construction, flight (+10 DB), Shield, size difference
```
```
(+5DB for each Size smaller than attacker), miscella-
```
neous bonus items and Adrenal Defense or Movement
skills and talents, otherwise DB is only the Quickness
```
(Qu/Qu/Qu) Stat.
```
In addition to the listed Defensive Bonus, most crea-
tures are capable of moving to avoid attacks. This is
reflected by Dodging, which provides a DB bonus
```
based on the applicable movement skill (e.g., Running
```
for creatures moving along the ground, Flying for
```
creatures in the air). See RMCL section 9.6 for details.
```
16
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Creature Law
HITS
Hits represent the ability to tolerate damage as per
body development skill with the addition of Co/Co/
SD stat bonuses. Each variety also adds a racial talent:
Basic Hits. The Size rules come into play, by modify-
ing a creature’s hits based on its size difference from
```
Medium (if applicable, per RMCL, Table 9-1). This
```
adjustment has already been calculated into the Hits
for all creatures, which are shown relative to the stan-
```
dardized (Medium) Character size. Body Development
```
```
also determines a creature’s Endurance; add the racial
```
Basic Endurance to the Body Development skill. This
result may be added to Endurance maneuvers, togeth-
er with applicable modifiers from circumstances, and
accumulated Fatigue penalties. To determine the Hits
before death: add the body development rank bonus
again to the total hits, to figure the number of Hits a
Character or Creature can go below zero Hits before
dying.
```
POWER POINTS (PP)
```
This skill is hard to develop for most archetypes, so
only spell user archetypes have consistently developed
ranks. Here it lists the result of the power development
skill. Modifiers from stats are double the Realm stat
```
(see Archetype) and one Co (Rs*/Rs*/Co). For Hybrid
```
Spell users use the lower of the 2 Realm stats.
SKILLS
Each creature has been assigned the most appropri-
ate skills, with the number of ranks dependent on the
Archetype and whether the skill is deemed Primary,
Secondary, or Tertiary. The skill bonus listed includes
the rank bonus, stat bonuses, and any modifiers from
talents, items, and other sources. This list of skills cov-
ers the basic requirements of the creature, but the GM
should feel free to add additional skills as appropriate
for the circumstances of the creature.
SPELL CASTING
If Creatures are able to cast spells like a spell user,
instead of their abilities being innate, the GM needs to
examine the PP and Spells section, after determining
casting level of the Creature and its Realm of magic.
Usually, they can be deduced from the Creature’s Type
and Variety. Determining Level is the easiest: simply
look at the Creature’s Level. This indicates what Lev-
el spells it might use and what Level is used as the
```
attack Level for Resistance Rolls (RR), as found in
```
Spell Law.
Determining Realm is mostly dictated by the role of
the Creature. Essence users will be potential damage
dealers and great miscellaneous helpers. Channeling
users can usually heal and be able to affect nature.
Mentalism users will be beneficial to themselves,
mostly, being able to heal and defend themselves and
also provide some attack options.
All of these details will be provided within the Stat
Block and Description for the specific creature. For
details on how these details are determined or for cus-
tomization options, see Creature Law II.
2.6. talents
Talents are what make a creature a monster, if they are
outside the realm of realism. All creatures have talents
```
and flaws; player characters and non-player characters
```
alike. A sampling of talents can be found in RMCL,
for use with characters, and these may also be applied
to creatures. Abilities that go beyond what is possi-
ble for characters, which are the exclusive domain of
creatures, are presented in Section 4. Additional tal-
ents and flaws will be included in Creature Law II.
Talents cost DP, just as for characters, with creatures
and monsters paying the same currency as characters.
Some talents might be innate spells, others might be
glands, and others might be extra limbs. Every crea-
ture has a certain number of talents assigned, and these
are listed in the entries for each creature.
17
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Creature LawChapter 3:Chapter 3:
Creatures StatisticsCreatures Statistics
18
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Creature Law
3.1. stat Blocks
We will be using a standardized Stat Block system for
displaying creature combat information within each
section of this and the next Creature Law volumes,
as shown on the following page printed in a landscape
style to allow for all of the necessary combat and en-
counter related information to be displayed.
Additional useful and descriptive information will be
listed for each specific creature as well within each
section of the book. Further information that will be
relevant to the Archetype and customization of crea-
tures will also be included within Creature Law II, to
allow GMs to modify the creatures from both books
as well as to create their own custom and balanced
creatures.
This encompasses any statistic a GM might need to
run the monster appropriately. All the basics, but also
the advanced stats are covered and Creature Talent
Chapter 4 also gives a good description of anything
you might need to know about the creature. Think of
it as a tiny character sheet and probably anything you
would ever need is there, except individual spells.
Writing down a monster in such detail might seem like
a lot of trouble, but the stat block remains the same
for this zombie, or for his master the lich, or for any
NPC and monster essentially. Based on the Creature
```
Category, Type and Variety, the Archetype table (ap-
```
```
pearing in Creature Law II section 2.2), the assigned
```
stats, and the creativity of the GM, all creatures can be
defined in this way.
```
Category: Lists the overarching Creature classifica-
```
tion. Section 2.1 explains this further. The creature can
be found in the Creature Law chapter based on the
Category.
```
Type: This is a further subdivision of creature clas-
```
sification. Section 2.1 may be consulted for a fuller
explanation.
COMBAT STAT BLOCK
Name/Variety: Specific creature example. Look in
section 2.1 Classifications for a full description. Short
version is that it is a member of a specific species of
creature.
```
Level: The foremost important statistic for any crea-
```
ture in Creature Law is Level. This defines most of its
offensive and defensive power, how high its values are
for its Skills and attacks and at what Level it uses its
spells, spell-like powers and other talents like disease
transmission and poison glands. Creatures of the aver-
age Level of the Player Character party are considered
to be on par with the Player Characters in the party,
providing a challenge to the players in most cases.
Just as for Player Characters, Level defines how much
a Creature has learned from its experiences. Direct
use of Level occurs mostly during RRs and when de-
termining parameters of spell and Talent effects. This
makes Level very important. Next to the Archetype
```
(as detailed in Creature Law II), Creature Category,
```
Type and Variety, the level is what puts numerical
meat on a Creature’s bones.
As part of balancing using DP to build creatures sim-
ilar to player characters, some creatures will have a
```
Level of 0 (zero), which indicates a half-level. This
```
can be thought of as the creature having DP for their
base Culture and Race, but not the DPs for 1st level
```
(which in terms of a Player Character would be the
```
```
equivalent of a very young child). For purposes of
```
spells and other effects which affect a fixed number of
```
levels (e.g., the Sleep III spell which affects 3 levels of
```
```
targets), treat such creatures as one half of a level (e.g.,
```
```
six level 0 creatures count as three levels of targets).
```
3. Creature Statistics3. Creature Statistics


Creature Law
Level Spread: This is meant to define Level spread
for Creatures encountered. It indicates how much the
Levels might differ within the creature group. Some
GMs might look at it and choose a Level based on
the spread, others might roll for each Creature, while
some disregard the spread. Note that it is only provid-
ed as a guide. GMs who want to detail creatures well
outside of the range shown can do so, especially if the
Creature Customization rules in Creature Law II are
in use.
```
Archetype: Section 2.2 explains archetypes. They are
```
like a profession for a creature. Archetype tables will
provide scores for the creature, per Level in Creature
Law II.
```
Outlook: This indicates how this Creature will react
```
to outside influences. It is linked to how the Creature
lives and will react to the outside world, but also deter-
mines in broad strokes how it will have developed its
skills. Depending on any natural Talents, the outlook
will determine largely in which archetype the Creature
can be classified.
Outlook Types
```
Aggressive: The Creature is actively looking to com-
```
mit violent acts. It will provoke others, impose itself
on more timid beings and reacts forcefully to any
provocation. The Offensive Archetype is most prob-
ably its path of development. Sometimes the Stealth
Archetype might be more appropriate.
```
Aloof: The aloofness usually has a good reason. Ei-
```
ther the being knows it can easily escape, or it is so
powerful that others pose no more threat than a fly
to a human. Interaction is useless because the being
doesn’t care, or it will only check to see if violence is
imminent. The Defensive Archetype applies here, or
perhaps the Spell caster Archetype.
```
Altruistic: Altruism is not very common, because
```
of the survival of the fittest principle. Those that are
altruistic are either rich beyond the wildest dreams,
or have abilities that grant them freedom from nor-
mal needs, so they are free to help others. They do
this even if that means going against their own goals,
or sacrificing some of their wealth. The Discipline
Archetype is not a bad way to go here, although the
Hybrid or Spell user Archetypes might be used.
```
Belligerent: This being is actively seeking to impose
```
its will on others by any means, often resorting to
violence. It will attack without provocation, or react
in a bad way to most interactions. The Offensive Ar-
chetype might do well, or the Stealth Archetype.
```
Berserk: The creature is either mindless, or driven
```
insane and will indiscriminately attack anything it
perceives as living, or a threat. The Offensive Arche-
type is typical, as no other Archetype’s abilities will
be used.
```
Carefree: Carefree demeanor is a sign of control.
```
The Creature is convinced of its own superiority,
imagined or real. It will, however, react to interac-
tions, with appropriate measures. Discipline Arche-
types might be carefree, or Skill Archetypes, when in
their normal environment.
```
Considerate: Beings that adhere to a considerate
```
demeanor help others and believe in the greater good
of all. They are positive and helpful and will resort
only to violence if provoked on many occasions.
Skill Archetypes might be considerate. Semi spell
user Archetypes as well.
```
Cruel: This demeanor is most rotten. Not only does
```
the creature actively seek violence, it delights in
inflicting pain, suffering and sadness. Stealth Arche-
type fit in this demeanor, but Offensive archetypes
might be cruel as well.

ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Creature Law
Archetypes almost by definition fall in this demean-
or, although Offensive archetypes fit well too.
```
Inquisitive: This creature is actively looking to
```
learn more. This says nothing of its motive, but it
will intrude on any business, private or no. Also it
will be initiating interaction at every possibility. Skill
Archetypes might be disposed such, but also perhaps
Semi spell users.
```
Jumpy: Jumpy creatures will extensively scan their
```
surroundings and anything that startles them, or
seems threatening, will cause them to turn tail and
bolt. Defensive Archetypes mostly are of this dispo-
sition. Skill Archetypes might react the same.
```
Normal: Having a normal demeanor means that
```
the Creature neither bolts at the first sign of danger,
neither charges headlong in every fight, but is some-
where in between. Most beings leading an ordinary
life will be normally disposed. Skill and discipline
Archetypes fit well enough.
```
Obsessive: Beings on a quest are very focused on
```
achieving it. Hindering them might lead to violent
interactions, and helping them might incur their
friendship. They usually cannot talk of anything else
besides their obsession and distractions from it are
viewed with distrust. Discipline and Semi spell user
Archetypes might be obsessively following a quest
most often.
```
Open: The open demeanor is about exchange of
```
information. They don’t mind questions, asked and
asking. They can be too inquisitive, but will not press
on if rebuffed. Discipline and Semi spell user Arche-
types are most likely to have this demeanor.
```
Passive: Passive creatures just wait and see what
```
happens. They somehow aren’t reacting fully to
outside influences. They will react against violence,
but little else moves them. Defensive archetypes are
sometimes passive. As are Spell user Archetypes.
```
Playful: Normally youthful, or fey, these creatures
```
want to have fun, all the time. For them its play,
for others it might be more serious. Interactions are
mostly fun, embarrassing, or painful and might re-
solve in hostilities. Spell user and Semi spell user
Archetypes are likely to have a playful demeanor.
```
Protective: Protective demeanor is where a being
```
has something that is valued and wants to protect
it. Sometimes they are under a geas to stand watch
and sometimes it is a mother protecting her babies.
Actions that deal with approaching and/ or taking
the protected subject, lead to violence. Defensive
Archetypes are sometimes considered protective, or
Offensive archetypes.
```
Crusading: These creatures are out to get some-
```
thing big done. They feel everything and everyone
is involved and they will be blatantly expecting aid
and alliance, and not being given that, take it as their
right. They are hard to reason with, unless their goal
is parallel to one’s own. Discipline or Semi spell user
Archetypes might fit this bill.
```
Dominant: Creatures that display dominant behav-
```
ior seek to bend others to their will. Threats of vio-
lence, or perhaps magic powers are frequent to cow
others to their will. In the end they might execute
their threats for real. Spell users of any type might be
like this, but Offensive Archetypes also display this
kind of behavior.
```
Friendly: Seeks to befriend beings, without any ul-
```
terior motives or desires other than companionship
and talk. Spell user Archetypes might be appropriate
or Defensive ones.
```
Greedy: Greed is the root of all evil, but basic greed
```
is human nature. Greedy beings covet what they have
not and might resort to any trick to get it, from barter
to stealing to outright murder. The Stealth Archetype
is most appropriate, but also the Skill Archetype.
```
Helpful: The helpful demeanor is all about helping
```
others, but within reason. Also, it usually has its own
goals that coincide with those being aided. Skill
Archetype, or Spell user Archetype fit well in this
demeanor.
Hidden agenda: This Outlook displays some other
demeanor than its actual demeanor, to mislead, or
trick others, or it might be to escape persecution.
Stealth Archetypes typically belong in this demeanor,
but Spell users of any kind could have hidden agen-
das. They pursue these, regardless of the situation.
```
Hostile: This demeanor is very dangerous. Violence
```
ensues on any interaction and it differs from berserk
only that it is not a state of mindless slaughter. Offen-
sive Archetype only.
```
Hungry: This being has a normal disposition, but
```
when it is hungry, it turns into something different....
It will probably hunt anything down that seems edi-
ble. Offensive, or skill Archetypes could be like this.
```
Hunting: These beings are looking for something
```
that is on the move, such as game, or criminals. They
will be asking questions, or examining their sur-
roundings and then leaving unless they have found
their quarry. Stealth and Semi spell user Archetypes
are possibly hunting.
```
Inconsiderate: Being inconsiderate is more a way
```
of living. Trying to get on top by any means. Such
creatures do not care about the suffering of others
and will tread down anything in their path. Stealth22
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Creature Law
```
Secretive: The creature has a secret it needs to hide
```
and will do anything, like posing as some other de-
meanor, to keep it. The nature of the secret could be
anything, but the deception is always there. Spell
user Archetypes are notoriously secretive as are
Stealth archetypes.
```
Timid: Shy and unforthcoming, this demeanor
```
makes beings shy away from interaction. They re-
spond mostly with silence and will back off at every
opportunity, especially when confronted with vio-
lence. Defensive archetypes are likely to be timid, as
are Skill Archetypes.
```
Example: Pixies have been assigned Playful as their
```
demeanor. The GM decides he needs a more serious
pixie NPC, who occasionally can’t resist the temp-
tation of pulling a prank on those around him. He
assigns the demeanor Inquisitive, to indicate a desire
for the Pixie to interact, not pull pranks all the time,
and get a chance to get acquainted with the PC party
in order to serve his storyline.
```
Movement: Mode of movement such as flying, swim-
```
ming and/or walking, including Base Movement Rate.
In all cases each creature has at least one mode of
locomotion, even if it is Rooted. The modes give a
base movement rate to work from, as modified shown
below. This is related to creature size, its Quickness
and how many legs it uses for movement. Four legs
is quite efficient, while having more legs provides
a smaller and smaller boost. Base rate indicates the
amount of feet traversed during a round of movement
```
at walking pace. Pace multipliers (maximum pace ap-
```
```
pears in parenthesis) apply when the creature is trying
```
to move faster, or slower. Using the assigned move-
ment talents, the base rate can be calculated using the
following formula: Base Movement for movement
talent + 1'/ rnd per 3" difference from 6' + Qu bonus/2
```
(once) + any modifiers from movement and Form
```
Talents. Note that Maneuver Bonus is included in the
Skills listing, further to the right in the Stat Block.
For modes of locomotion the creature is not adapted
for, or there is no movement listed, assume the crea-
ture operates as if the Awkward Movement IV move-
ment flaw is applicable: reduce the movement rate to
25% of their main BMR. Example: a human has only
```
its bipedal movement listed; so if he goes climbing or
```
swimming, his movement rate is decreased to 25% and
he is limited to Jog maximum pace. However, apply
-25 to his standard movement maneuver total, unless
he has developed the applicable movement skill.
23
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Creature Law
Exhaustion/Endurance: This score is the bonus used
for Endurance rolls, as described in RMCL section
5.5. In summary, successful Endurance rolls result in
no fatigue while failure results in a -10 fatigue penalty
```
(-5 for partial success) which applies to all actions.
```
```
Fatigue is easily remedied by resting (penalties reduce
```
```
by 1 per minute) but any penalty beyond -50 becomes
```
```
an injury penalty (e.g., sprained ankle, stretched ten-
```
```
don) which will take much longer to heal. A creature
```
which would fail an Endurance roll has the option to
```
cancel the action (deciding it is too tired to continue),
```
but depending on the circumstances that could mean
giving up a chase or failing to escape.
```
Size: The creature’s comparative size is listed here.
```
Medium indicates a creature 6' tall and about 200 lbs.
in weight. Medium is the basic size. Larger creatures
will use larger attacks and might get bulk and damage
bonuses. Smaller creatures might have an easier time
hiding, but also suffer penalties due to lack of weight in
Feats of Strength contests, cannot use larger items, and
suffer more damage from larger attacks. The Increased
and Decreased Size Talents will be found among the
Talents that modify this. Dense/Light Boned, Superi-
or/Inferior Stat are others. Weight determines the size
category of the creature, although not necessarily the
damage and critical modifications that apply.
High Elven Paladin Nin is a very statuesque female
of the species. Her weight, due to her martial train-
ing and remarkable size approaches the threshold
for becoming Big. However, since her race does not
provide the Increased Size Talent, she can never
progress in size category, even if she started a fried
bread only diet.

ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Creature Law
```
Critical Code (Crit): This is where special critical
```
modifications are listed, such as extra critical modi-
fications and damage results a creature might ignore.
Talents will dictate which modifiers will be found here.
The first indicator is the roman numeral for the size
```
and critical modifications (with V indicating Medi-
```
```
um). Compare the size of the attack against this value
```
to determine any modification to the critical severity
```
(as listed on the Size Difference Adjustments table).
```
This value may be followed by additional codes which
are listed on the Critical Codes table, e.g., “VII, @#”
indicates a creature that will suffer attacks as a Large
```
(Size 7) creature, which further is immune to stun (@)
```
```
and bleeding (#).
```
```
Hits: These are the basic unit of how much physical
```
punishment the creature can take. Hits are based on
```
the creature’s talents, stats, and size (larger creatures
```
```
having more hits, as detailed in RMCL section 9.4).
```
The table shows the number of hits followed by the
```
number required to kill the creature (in parentheses).
```
The creature will fall unconscious when its hits are
reduced to zero, but will not die until the total damage
```
exceeds the number in parentheses (at which point the
```
creature has reached negative hits beyond their Body
```
Development skill bonus).
```
```
Armor Type (AT): This represents the first line of
```
defense of the creature. AT stands for Armor Type and
indicates the protective value of the creature’s armor
or hide. Creatures with no meaningful natural protec-
tion, such as Humans, have an AT of 1, representing
naked skin or light clothes. Creatures with tougher
hides, carapaces, or other natural protection gain pro-
tection equivalent to wearing armor, although with
none of the associated encumbrance. Typical ATs for
different types of natural defenses are given on Table
3-2a in the next section. Creatures with natural armor
also wearing actual armor gain only the better of the
two AT values, or the Piecemeal Armor rules can be
used if this improved armor only covers parts of the
creature’s body.
```
Defensive Bonus (DB): DB is the numerical value
```
deducted from the attack roll of someone trying to
```
hit. The value given includes Quickness DB (3x Qu
```
```
bonus), the DB bonus for flying targets, and DB from
```
any talents that operates at all times.
In addition, the second line, in square brackets, gives
the creature’s Running ranks followed by the total
```
Running bonus (or ranks plus bonus for the creature’s
```
primary movement skill, e.g., Flying for flying crea-
```
tures). A creature aware of attacks may make a Passive
```
```
Dodge (add their Running ranks to their DB) or may
```
```
choose to make a Partial or Full Dodge (see RMCL
```
```
section 9.6). Dodging is not included in the base DB,
```
since it depends on the creature’s actions for the round.
The third line, in parenthesis, indicates the type of
shield used by the creature, if any, as well as the num-
ber of Shield ranks and Shield skill bonus. A creature
with a shield may choose to make a Passive Block
```
(add their Shield ranks to their DB) or a Partial or Full
```
```
Block (see RMCL section 9.6).
```
It should also be noted that if the creature is aware, it
can elect to use OB as DB, using parrying, or it might
elect to use Cover, or even Protect someone else. Oth-
er DB bonuses may come from Talents and items. If a
shield is normally used, the additional shield DB will
be shown in parentheses. Most DB, including Quick-
ness DB, Dodging, and Shields, does not apply against
attacks the creature is not aware of.
```
Initiative: Added to the initiative roll is the initiative
```
value. The initiative value is derived from the crea-
ture’s quickness stat bonus, and any applicable talents,
such as Fast Attack. Other situational modifiers may
apply like injury penalties.
Table 3.1e - Critical Codes
Code Explanation
I
Creature reduces the severity of any critical by 1. This
is in addition to any critical modifications due to Size
difference.
!
This creature cannot be staggered, knocked back, or
knocked down, due to its construction, physical make up,
or a special ability.
@
Creature is immune to stun results. It suffers only a -25
```
penalty for the remainder of the round (next round if it
```
```
has no Action Points left), not cumulative with other such
```
results.
# Creature is immune to bleeding results. Convert every hitof bleeding to a concussion hit.
PG
No Grapple: This creature cannot be grappled. It takes
half damage from grappling and wrestling attacks, and is
unaffected by Grapple criticals.
PK No Krush: Creature takes half damage from crushingtypes of attacks, and is unaffected by Krush criticals.
PP No Punctures: Creature takes half damage from punctur-ing attacks, and is unaffected by Puncture criticals.
PS No Slash: Creature takes half damage from slashingattacks, and is unaffected by Slash criticals.
PT No Strikes: Creature takes half damage from unarmedstrikes attacks, and is unaffected by Strikes criticals.
PU
No Unbalancing: Creature takes half damage from
unbalancing attacks, and is unaffected by Unbalancing
criticals.
25
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Creature Law
```
Attacks: Attacks come from many sources, such as
```
talents, form and as acquired weapons.
Animal attacks are listed with a size of the attack letter
```
first, capitalized; being Mi (1), D (2), T (3), S (4), M
```
```
(5), B (6), L (7), H (8), G (9), E (10), I (11), O (12),
```
```
V (13), followed by two or three letters indicating the
```
attack type. Weapons and spell attacks are indicated
in exactly the same way: Size letter first with the size
class number in parenthesis, then a two or three letter
abbreviation for the used attack table. Some attacks
will show a [crit type] note after the type of attack,
which indicates that if a critical is scored on the attack
that an additional critical of the type specified is also
applied using the same critical roll for the normally
occurring critical type.
Attack directions: In the statistics block there are a
number of modifiers that will direct the GM to spe-
cifically apply the attacks. First of all: the order of the
attacks. The first attack is either the most prominent
unarmed attack of the creature or the primary weapon
OB. The second number is either the second most im-
portant unarmed, or the secondary weapon OB. From
there the GM is free to apply the attacks, except when
there are indications after an attack, see below:
Attack sequence additions
> — the single arrow indicates the attack sets the
victim up for a second attack the next round, if the
first attack yields a critical result. This attack will
```
be treated as an instantaneous action (0 AP, with
```
```
no penalty for the reduced AP). When the first
```
attack is a grappling attack, achieving a critical is
not enough to initiate the next attack. The creature
needs to achieve 100% grappling percentage to be
able to use the next attack.
>> — the double arrow means that the next listed
attack may be initiated without spending AP in the
same round, if the first listed attack achieves a crit-
ical result. This critical result must result in rolling
at least an A critical on the victim. This means that
larger creatures are less subject to such follow up
attacks. This is due to a natural order of a com-
pound attacks, such as a horn ram or a grab and
crush. Sometimes attack sequences might be from
attack modes such as pouncing or Unbalancing at-
tacks. In case this symbol appears after a grappling
attack, a critical is not enough to trigger it, but the
creature needs to engage in grappling, to achieve a
100% incapacitation of the victim, adding together
the wound penalties and Grappling percentage,
after which the next attack is initiated.
As per Core Law 7.1, Trample attacks should only
be allowed if the Attacker is at least 2 size categories
larger than the target.
Or as an optional Creature Law rule, Trample attacks
may occur when the Attacker is +1 size Larger if the
target has been knocked prone.
Table 3.1f - Attack Designations
Attack size Natural/Magical attacks Weapon attacks
Abbr Explan. Abbr Explan. Abbr Explan. Abbr Explan.


Creature Law
coopeRation additions
Coop## — This behind an attack signifies that the
creature, if it comes in sufficient numbers may
jointly attack their victim. If it has the talent: Co-
```
operation: Flock/ Pack Attack, the creatures will
```
add a bonus to their joint attack and may increase
the size of their attack by one or more. If the
creatures have Cooperation: strength in number,
they confuse their foe who gets a -25 to all actions
when the ## number of creatures engage it. With
the Cooperation: Pick Up and Drop talent, the
creatures may use the attack listed to grab their
prey and air lift it. They will fly up and deliver
it somewhere, or drop it back to the earth from a
```
great height (600' is about enough to achieve max-
```
```
imum velocity for a human body).
```
multiple attack additions
#WF — An entry such as 2wf or 3wf indicates that
the creature can make the specified number of
attacks per round, using the preceding attacks but
at the OB given for the #wf entry. In some cases
multiple entries will give different options, such as
“any two weapons” or “any three weapons.” The
OB listed is based on the creature’s normal OB
```
modified by the Kata rules (RMCL section 9.7) and
```
```
the creature’s Multiple Attacks skill (and in some
```
```
cases by talents).
```
```
Offensive Bonus (OB): This lists the Offensive Bonus
```
of the creature with a given attack type, reflecting the
creature’s level, archetype, stats, and talents.
```
Power Points (PP): Power points are derived from the
```
Power Development skill and the Innate Power talent.
Power points are expended to cast spells and also to
use spell-like abilities from applicable talents.
```
Resistance Roll (RR):
```
```
RR (C)hanneling/ RR (E)ssence/ RR (M)ental-
```
```
ism/ RR (P)hysical/ RR (F)ear:
```
Here the Resistance Roll bonuses are recorded. The
listed bonuses take into account racial modifiers, stat
bonuses, applicable talents, and the creature’s level. In
```
addition, every creature has an assigned realm (just as
```
```
for characters) and receives a +10 bonus when resist-
```
ing spells from that realm, and this is also included in
the total.
```
Skill: With Rolemaster’s skill system being a central
```
component to its flexibility, creatures can develop a
wide range of the skills that characters can develop
from RMCL. Many of the character skills won’t be
```
relevant to most creatures, but some will; especially so
```
for intelligent creatures. We have not applied Knacks
```
to any of the creatures (RMCL, page 83 & 244). This
```
allows GMs to easily modify each of the creatures in
```
an encounter, by adding bonuses to a couple (or more)
```
skills of their choosing according to the Power Level
```
used in their game (see section 2.3).
```
```
Biomes: Creatures have places that they live. Some
```
have a preferred climate, others a preferred terrain for
survival. The information presented here classifies
creatures according to Biomes and Locations. This
table is slightly expanded from what is found in Core
Law Table 15-1, as summarized here for convenience.
Note, some additions have been added due to special-
ized areas that are not included in the Biomes used
for skill designations, due to their unusual nature.
Climates are loosely defined, with Cold being areas
where below freezing temperatures are the norm for
more than 30% of the year, Tropical temperatures al-
most never see temperatures drop down to freezing,
while Temperate climates fall between these two
conditions. Note however that these are only loose
guidelines and the GM should feel free to modify as
per their needs or world concept.
27
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Creature Law
Number Encountered: This is mostly a logical num-
ber range indication, such as social group size, or so-
loist tendencies. It is better to assign how many beings
are encountered, to keep the challenging factor in line
with player-character capabilities.
```
Frequency: This statistic is given to indicate the over-
```
all rarity of the Creature in its Biomes. It is an indi-
cation of how populous the Creature is in its natural
habitat. Extraneous factors apply to heighten or lower
the frequency rating. It may actually be found outside
its climate comfort zone, but it will be even more
rare than is indicated here. If someone is specifically
```
looking for the Creature; add the Frequency difficulty
```
modifier to the appropriate Lore maneuver, Tracking
maneuvers to locate such a creature, or Survival for
```
hunting (knowing where to look for tracks or spoor for
```
```
hunting the creature).
```


Creature Law
3.2. cReatuRe details
```
The following details will be included (where rele-
```
```
vant) within the text descriptions of each creature.
```
CREATURE GROUPINGS
Within the creature categories, creatures will be divid-
ed into like groups. There will be a description that
describes the group as a whole.
```
Description: Here one can read more about the crea-
```
ture. Its habits, favorite tactics, breeding and feeding
habits. Sometimes small clues about attacks and spe-
cial talents are given here. Ranges are sometimes giv-
en, often as range increments in angle brackets such
```
as <5’/lvl>. The number listed is Short range (incur-
```
```
ring no penalty). From this distance up to twice the
```
```
increment is Medium Range (with -10 to OB). Further
```
```
increments of the distance give Long (-25), Extreme
```
```
(-50), and Absurd (-100) attack ranges. No attack be-
```
yond Absurd range is possible.
GMs should note that any value found in the descrip-
tions is ultimately subject to his own judgement.
```
Example: The GM looks up the flying speed of a Mo-
```
loch demon. It is listed as 66' per round. He decides
to use 65' per round because his battlemat is divided
in 5' hexes.
The same is true for talents and other abilities present-
ed in the description. Sometimes the GM may need to
adjudicate which talents are available to a creature at a
certain time and which it may use.
In the case of shapechangers use which talent seems
more appropriate. Most shapechangers may change
to a half form and use as many talents as they have
paid for. Such half forms are totally subject to GM
discretion.
```
Example: In a bizarre twist of fate, Rockmelter Suzi
```
has become half Sstoi’ss’lithi, half Dwarf. The two
forms are very incompatible so the GM rules that
she cannot become a dwarf with poison fangs nor a
snake with hair and other mammalian features. She
pouts a little but accepts the GM’s ruling.
DETAILED STAT BLOCK
```
Category: The overarching category which the crea-
```
ture belongs to, as described in section 2.1.
```
Archetype: The general framework of the creature’s
```
development, equivalent to a profession, as described
in section 2.2.
```
Variants: When the listed creature encompasses
```
multiple species, they may be listed as variants. For
example, “Bird, Diminutive” provides stats for most

ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Creature Law
Every bit of treasure generated by random rolls is
completely subject to GM discretion.
```
Example: A zombie has an individual treasure of “a”
```
Wealth Code and “l” Lifestyle Code. The GM rolls
```
43 for wealth rolls (TL, 2 rolls on TL, he generates
```
```
2-20 (13 sp coins) and 8-80 (62 sp coins), multi-
```
plied by 0.01 this gives a total of 7 bp and 5 cp. He
rolls a 50 on TL, -5 equipment. It seems the zombie
was made from the unfortunate corpse of a rather
poor farmer’s hand with some lousy equipment. No
shocker there.
birds in this general size category, and lists as exam-
ples “Crow, Raven, Woodpecker.” Creatures with no
such variants may omit this entry.
```
Size: Height, length, and weight provide a sense of
```
scale and are relevant for determining melee range and
encumbrance. When specific values are listed they are
averages for the whole gamut of lengths, heights and
weights for all the creatures. In some cases, however,
a range of heights, lengths, or weights is provided es-
pecially when adult males and females differ greatly
```
in size (in some species the males will be larger, in
```
```
others the females). Variability may indicate some
```
individuals are outside the normal range for their Size
```
category; Creature Law II will discuss how to adjust
```
the Size if desired.
```
Height (inches): for bipedal Creatures this is mea-
```
sured to the head. For other creatures this is listed
to the withers. It usually indicates the length of the
stride.
```
Length (inches): viewed from the side, this is the
```
measurement between the front and the back. Usu-
ally this does not include a head and tail.
```
Weight (lbs.): weights are listed in pounds. They
```
have a profound effect on the damage done and re-
ceived by the creature.
```
Armor: This provides a description of the creature’s
```
natural armor or armor worn if it normally uses a man-
ufactured armor. Especially low natural AT creatures
may use armoring that can be put on. These fall in
the same classifications as armors in the RMCL. The
creature needs to develop this as a skill to reduce the
penalty imparted by wearing the armor. Wearing ar-
mor over natural armor doesn’t increase AT. Simply
use the higher AT, and if the natural AT is lower, add
+1 DB for each AT it is above 1.
```
Treasure (individual/lair): This gives the average
```
treasure a member of the variety is likely to own, ei-
ther on its person, or in a lair, somewhere. The notation
will be in a series of letters with the letters indicating
treasure rolls as given by the random treasure tables
```
in Treasure Law (TL) section 7.1. The Wealth rolls
```
allow multiple rolls, a lifestyle roll will indicate qual-
ity of living quarter furnishings and gear, and magical
wealth simply gives a value of magic items the crea-
ture may possess.
Individual treasure is what is carried or perhaps ab-
sorbed or swallowed by a single individual, while lair
treasure is held up in the lair or feeding place, either
just lying about, or hoarded by the group of creatures
in the lair. Usually only the most powerful creature in
the lair has lair treasure. It would be normal for intel-
ligent specimen to loan out items to powerful under-
lings, or pay hirelings from the treasure.

ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Creature Law
Material/Size: Some types of creatures, especially
those that are artificial and animated can have varying
```
weights depending on their materials (which affects
```
```
their size category as per RMCL section 9.4). Materi-
```
als have been taken into account for creatures in this
volume, but if it is desired to assess how other materi-
als would vary, use the following guidelines:
• Look up a creature that has a very similar body
structure, then:
```
• (Length New divided by Length Old) raised to
```
the third power times Weight Old gives Weight
New.
• If a creature is made of a different material,
```
then use the relative specific weights (relative
```
```
to flesh) to calculate the final weight by simply
```
multiplying the resulting weight in flesh by the
number in the table below. The mass of flesh is
slightly above that of water for most creatures
```
(humans for example average 63.1 lbs/Cu.ft.).
```
It should also be noted that the values below
for various types of woods are rough estimates
```
when “dry” (moisture content of 12%), whereas
```
when wet or “green” can be up to +16% times
```
(~30% moisture content) the density given in
```
Table 3.2b below.
• To account for morphological differences be-
tween different sizes, add 10% per size category
above Medium.
• Also note that some mechanical creatures are
not actually solid and thus may have void
spaces within their structure, possibly decreas-
ing their actual mass by between 10% and 50%
```
(subject to GM discretion).
```
Healing rate: Creature’s healing rate is listed here. It
might be faster, or slower than a common human’s x1
wound recovery multiplier. The modifier listed indi-
cates the modification to the time of recovery, so x2
```
means: creature’s wounds take twice as long to heal.
```
x0.5 means twice as fast, or half as long, which is the
same. It applies also to concussion hit recovery.
```
Realm: The creature’s likely Realm is noted for many
```
purposes. It may differ, except for certain Spell user
Archetypes, who get assigned specific Realms. Realm
determines which stat bonus to use for PP calculations,
where the Creature may choose its spells, if any and
the RR bonus of +10 to own Realm.
```
Miscellaneous(Misc.): This will be just additional
```
tidbits of information on the creature described in De-
tailed Stat Block.
Stat Bonuses: This shows the stat bonuses for the
creature in question. This is a combination of the
creatures’ base racial stat bonuses as well as any ad-
justments for the archetype level of the creature and
any other stat bonuses that the creature has through
talents, flaws or any other means. While these val-
ues generally won’t be relevant to most interactions
between these creatures and the characters during en-
counters, there is always a slim chance that they may
be useful in some way. However, these stat bonuses
will become very relevant when Creature Law II is
released, with details of how creatures can be cus-
tomized/modified by GMs, as these are the base stat
bonuses that can be customized by the GM for their
use in their settings.
```
Spells: Here are noted either several spells the Crea-
```
ture may use, or the total number of spells the Creature
has developed. These must be divided over spell lists
that the Creature has access to. Normally Spell Ranks
listed here may be picked from Base Lists and Open
lists one on one, while Hybrid and Pure Spell user Ar-
chetypes may convert two of the listed Spell Ranks to
one Closed spell, or 6 listed picks for one Restricted
spell. Semi spell users do not have this luxury and the
GM must figure out the conversion rate for each Semi
spell user type. GM should feel free to change spell
lists to fit their needs.
```
Talents: These are special abilities that a creature has
```
not trained for but was born with, or gained by follow-
ing a certain regimen, which then accustomed their
body to being able to use the specific talent. Talents
may add anything from within the realm of physical
reality, or outside those bounds, such as elemental
breaths or such a terrible visage as to turn people to
stone. Note that not all Talents may appear in this list,
```
as some of the talents are shown elsewhere (such as
```
Movement talents, or others that are obvious within
```
the stat block for the creature).
```
```
Description: This will be descriptive text of the indi-
```
vidual creature shown in the Detailed Stat Block.
