```
special modification of -20) or else not regain any lost
```
Hits, spent Power Points or fatigue penalty as long
as the Mara plays, and make a Physical RR or lose
2 points of Constitution bonus per night of disturbed
sleep. More disturbing yet, the Mara drains 1 point of
Constitution bonus per round from any living being
within a 10' radius who does not successfully make a
-10 Physical RR. Should the solitude of those asleep
be interrupted, the Mara flees to avoid detection. The
```
body of a Mara is insubstantial; thus magical weapons
```
are needed to harm them.
  
An insubstantial phantom which
flits along upon the night winds,
the Mara is hardly ever viewed
by the living, or if one is seen, it
is almost never recognized
as Undead. In life gifted with
song, the Mara uses its talent in
death to trouble the sleep of the
living. Bound to no particular
location, they make their abodes i n
dark attics or abandoned cellars of
houses inhabited by the living. In the
quiet hours of the night, these spirits
seek out slumbering creatures in order
to serenade them with eerie ballads on
ethereal instruments. While pleasing
to the Mara, the ghostly music is not
healthy to the sleeping listener. The Mara
is a Class II Undead. The sound of the
Mara’s phantom music brings nightmares
```
to sleepers within hearing range (up to
```
```
100’ away) without waking them and de-
```
stroys the restorative properties of the sleep.
```
The sleeper must make a Mentalism RR (with a
```

333
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Creature Law
These Undead are all immaterial haunts which appear
only partially in our world. Usually, only the head and
torso are completely visible, with the rest of the body
fading into apparent nonexistence. Also, most Reve-
nants clutch a weapon of some sort in a hand which
is not absolutely corporeal. Revenants prowl the night
in many areas, but are seemingly most concentrated
in areas where many have died suddenly or tragically.
However, they also have a particular fancy for cem-
eteries where visitors are rare. But, when an intruder
does become evident, a Revenant will attack it imme-
diately, attempting to quench another soul and bring
it into the darkness of death. With whatever weapon a
Revenant attacks with, it also delivers a Cold critical
of equal severity to any critical normally achieved
with a maximum severity of C. Additionally, these
Undead will drain 1 Constitution bonus per round to
any living being they touch who fail a Physical RR
```
(at -10). Finally, a Revenant has access to Sorcerer
```
spells. Due to their immaterial forms, magical weap-
ons are necessary to harm them. An artful suicide ties
this specter to the world. Death channels its inward
destruction toward the living, further strengthening
the chains of blood that bind its soul. The weapon that
ended its life, if such exists, accompanies the ghost
on its nightly forays to draw new victims through the
last gate.
These Undead are insubstantial spirits whose pale,
translucent faces display a contorted rictus of pain,
expressing their death agonies. The misty image of a
diseased or dismembered body sometimes accompa-
nies the horror of the face. Even though these Undead
are unaware of their origin, Loremasters contend that
they are generated upon the slow wasting deaths of
prisoners in deep, forgotten dungeon cells, or those
who have been locked away in high towers. Basically,
anyone who dies after years of imprisonment, torture,
and loss of all hope is a candidate for generating a
Phantom. The minds of these poor souls have already
been cracked and broken—only a will to find surcease
of pain remains, but these motivations only serve
to fuel the movements of these creatures. Phantoms
wander across the lands, always searching, but never
finding. They search for what they have lost but once
possessed while inhabiting the realm of the living:
flesh, emotion and memories. These Undead know not
their reason for being, and may not even realize that
they are no longer alive. The only motive which now
propels them is the desire for what can never again
be theirs. Phantoms are Class I Undead. In the event
of a conflict, the phantom strikes with its bare hands,
```
dealing an additional Electricity critical (maximum of
```
```
D severity), and on a successful attack also inflicts a
```
Cold Ball attack, as well as draining 1 Constitution
```
bonus per round (from those who fail a Physical RR at
```
```
-10 modification). The bodies of these restless spirits
```
are immaterial, thus only magical weapons will have a
normal effect upon them.
```
Phantom (I)Phantom (I)
```

334
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Creature Law
SHADOWS
Huge, cloudy bat wings augment the soaring flight
```
adapted by this spirit; a shred of Darkness barely visi-
```
ble against the night sky. Invisible fangs facilitate con-
sumption of the warm and living red blood of its prey
to satiate its raging hunger. Once human, the Shadow
favors mannish life fluids, transformed into a cannibal
by its unnatural existence. Indeed, without blood, it
will fade away to nothingness. Upon finding prey, it
```
can paralyze victims within 20’R (Minor Shadow) or
```
```
25’R (Greater Shadow) if they fail a Physical RR (as a
```
```
3 AP action usable no more than once every 6 rounds).
```
When foes are bitten, it leeches away an additional
```
5 (Minor Shadow) or 10 (Greater Shadow) hits if a
```
```
Physical RR (-10) is failed; this restores damage it
```
might have suffered.
Shadows will typically initiate attacks with a paralyz-
ing burst, within a 20’ radius for Minor Shadows and
```
25’ for Greater Shadows (those failing a Physical RR
```
```
are paralyzed and unable to move for 6 rounds). They
```
will then bite their prey, and if those bites deal damage
```
they will also leech 5 (Minor) or 10 (Greater) addi-
```
```
tional damage if a Physical RR at -10 is failed; leeched
```
damage restores the Shadows.
```
Shadow Minor (II)Shadow Minor (II)
```
```
Category: Undead, Incorporeal
```
```
Archetype: Stealth
```
```
Size: 5'9", weightless
```
```
Armor: insubstantial AT 1
```
```
Treasure: i
```
```
Realm: Channeling
```

AG CO EM IN ME PR QU RE SD ST

```

(5 DP); Leech, damage I [5 hits, -10] (15 DP); Abom-
```

```
ination (-20 DP); Regeneration II [1 H/ rnd, 10 min.]
```
```
(30 DP); Shadow (25 DP); Type II (-6 DP); Unsleeping
```
```
(20 DP); Restricted diet [Blood] (-5 DP); Mute (-10
```
```
DP); Eternal hunger [Blood, 1 day] (-20 DP); Link,
```
```
blood (10 DP); Life sense I [5'R/ lvl] (15 DP); Missing
```

SPECTERS
Cold white lights inhabiting the region where eyes
might shine reveal the presence of these nearly invis-
ible spirits. Terror heralds their arrival and fades with
the departure of the tattered cloaks they often wrap
around their non-corporeal forms. When a living be-
ing dies with a lust for power and evil heavy on their
souls, it may be that their ties to the earth will not be
severed upon death. Rather, these souls will gather
to them the essence of Unlife, forming the horribly
powerful Undead known as Specters. Once made
manifest, however, Specters seem woefully incapable
of gaining any true power, as if they were played for
some cruel joke. Instead, they must be satisfied with
performing evil deeds, which they take to with a ven-
geance. These immaterial haunts seek areas where the
presence of strong evil shadows the surrounding land
in weary despair. Their unrelenting lust for unclean
pursuits makes them a true terror for any living being
which might chance across their path. Minor Specters
are Class II Undead, Standard Specters are Class III
Undead, and Lord Specters are Class VI Undead.
All Specters generate fear within a varying radius of
```
their presence (2'/lvl at -20 for Minor, 3'/lvl at -30 for
```
```
Standard, and 4'/lvl at -40 for Lord), forcing those in
```
the area to make a Fear RR. Additionally, their in-
```
substantial touch drains Constitution bonus (1 from
```
```
Minor, 2 from Standard and 3 from Lord) for those
```
```
who fail their RR (with a special modification of -10).
```
```

```

ning Bolt attack, while the Standard can discharge a

Electricity critical of equal severity in addition to any
critical normally delivered. Also, Major Specters can
use spells as in their former life. All these creatures
have immaterial bodies such that magical weapons are
needed to damage them.

336
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Creature Law
  
The Swamp Star manifests as a small, bright light. It
hops from point to point, pulsing and changing color
in an attempt to gain the attention of those viewing it.
Experienced travelers in swamplands are full of horri-
fying stories concerning the naive and their deaths in
the heart of a murky fen after following a beckoning
light towards an unfulfilled promise of treasure. While
```
old swamp dogs (guides through swampy areas) can
```
chart a path through the wending, watery ways of
most any marsh, they too are wary of transgressing
into territories known to be haunted by Swamp Stars,
as their flashing lights can have a hypnotic affect on
even the strongest minded. Denizens of marches and
fens, Swamp Stars are notorious for leading travelers
off the proper paths into deep water and quicksand
where death awaits. It attempts to lead travelers astray
with its hopping motion, color changes, and changing
the intensity of its illumination. Those who are caught
by quicksand or submerged in murky water make a
quick feast for the Swamp Star as it quickly drains off
the life force of any struggling victim. The light of the
Swamp Star can hypnotically influence those who fail
an RR to follow it into dangerous waters. The touch
of this Undead will drain 2 Constitution bonus per
round from the bodies of the living who do not make
a successful -10 RR versus Physical each round. The
incorporeal form of the Swamp Star makes it hard to
damage them without the aid of magical weapons.
```
Specter, Minor (II)Specter, Minor (II)
```

337
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Creature Law
WIGHTS
These Undead appear as dark, shadowy human forms
with eyes akin to faint lights. Pale white limbs appear
now and again, but are immaterial and quickly hidden
again beneath their shadowy raiment. When observed
with the aid of magic, they take on the tattered forms of
great lords and ladies with cold, cruel gleaming eyes.
Burial mounds and cemeteries located near strong-
holds of powerfully evil beings never seem to fare
well. Evil influences creep across the land, filling up
dark valleys, swampy fens, and graveyards with foul,
unwholesome energies. When this unclean influence
saturates the final resting place of a once-noble lord or
lady, laid low by mortality, there is often nothing to pre-
vent a necromantic spirit from inhabiting the remains
of those interred there. These spirits are in some part
remnants of the those originally buried, so their ani-
mation is particularly depraved. Barrow-wights haunt
the mounded tombs erected to honor great warriors
and kings, while the other forms of this Undead haunt
abandoned graveyards and profaned cemeteries. They
roam the narrow halls and low tunnels of the tombs,
daily playing out the actions of their former lives, or
else sitting still for years at a time, contemplating the
evil fortune which brought them to their present state.
These Undead suffer no intrusions, dealing harshly
with any who might violate the tomb or its environs.
Standard Wights are Class IV Undead, Greater Wights
are Class IV, Major Wights are Class V Undead, and
Barrow-wights are Class VI Undead. Wights wield
with cold efficiency the enchanted weapons often
```
buried with the dead (an additional Cold critical of
```
equal severity is delivered with any normally achieved
```
critical). Anyone approaching too closely to a Wight
```
must make a Fear RR, within 42’ for Standard,68’ for
Greater, 100’ for Major, and 125’ for Barrow Wights.
Greater and Major Wights manifest illusions to simu-
late their former glory and confuse or terrify those who
intrude upon their resting places. Their touch drains 1
Constitution bonus per 10 failure on a Physical RR,
to a maximum of 1 for Standard, 2 for Greater and
Major, and 3 for Barrow Wights. The touch of Barrow
```
Wights additionally paralyzes (as Paralyze I from the
```
Mind Attack spell list, permitting a Mentalism RR to
```
resist). Due to their non-solid form, magical weapons
```
are necessary to harm them.
```
Wight, Major (V)Wight, Major (V)
```
```

```

339
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Creature Law
WRAITHS
A quivering in the air, a red glow where the eyes might
flash, a garment or armor housing empty air: these are
the signs of a Wraith. Its corporeal structure has been
so changed through foul magic that it hardly exists in
the physical world. Wraiths are the creation of super-
naturally evil Powers. Usually, a mortal selected to
become a Wraith is someone particularly powerful
or holds an important political position. Wraiths have
available to them all the spell lists they had learned
in life, which are usually extensive as Wraiths are
normally created from mortals already possessing
considerable power. The spell lists listed are an ex-
ample, but the GM should feel free to modify based
on their needs. The Wraith form confers many powers
upon these Undead as well. An opponent who fails a
```
Mentalism RR can be "held" (loses 3 AP per round)
```
```
by a Wraith's gaze (a free action for the Wraith as long
```
```
as the Wraith and target can see each other). Range
```
of this ability is 75' for a Greater Wraith and 110' for
a Major Wraith. Also, because their form is not com-
pletely solid on this plane, magical weapons are re-
quired to harm them. The Greater Wraith is surround-
ed by an Aura of fear within 45’, while a Major Wraith
Fear Aura has a 110’ radius, with normal fear effects.
Anyone within a 5' radius suffers from a Cold Critical
of A severity for a Greater or B severity for a Major
Wraith. They also deliver a Cold critical in addition
to any other critical they achieve during combat up to
an A Cold for Greater and B Cold for Major Wraiths.
Finally, their touch drains 1 Constitution bonus
per 10 failure on a Physical RR, up to a
maximum of 2 for Greater Wraiths and
3 for Major Wraiths. Many of these
Undead manifest slay-knives,
which leave fragments deep in
the wounds delivered by the
blade. The effect is the same
as the Constitution-draining
effect of their touch, once per
day. If death occurs from
this draining, a new
Wraith is created who
is under the control of
the Wraith which slew
him or her.
A pack of ghostly, white hounds whose eyes and
mouths, glowing red, paint racing streaks of pale fire
across the skies and the lands on cold, windy, moon-
less nights. Their wild cries drive all without shelter
before these tireless pursuers. Hunt Hounds are Class
II Undead. A pack of these Undead hounds generate
a Wave of fear one hundred feet in front of them
and those in the wave must make a Fear RR against
a level 10 Fear effect. This may change depending on
how the prey fares against the Hunt: Increase the Fear
level if the prey is losing and decrease the Fear level
if the prey is winning. When a pack descends upon
```
fleeing prey from the sky (these creatures run on
```
```
both land and air), each individual hound will not
```
```
pause to engage a victim; but rather bite deeply
```
once, then pass by to leave the laggard to the
fangs of the rest of the pack as it passes. Those
Hounds able to bite the victim without divert-
```
ing too far from the chase will do so (either in-
```
dividually or combining to make cooperative
```
pack attacks), while others may simply run past.
```
```
Those bitten must make a Physical RR (-10
```
```
modification) for each bite, failure indicating
```
1 Constitution bonus drained for every bite
they receive.
```
Wild Hunt Hound (II)Wild Hunt Hound (II)
```
```
Category: Undead, Incorporeal
```
```
Archetype: Offensive
```

```

```

-50343
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Creature Law
These creatures can do little but mindlessly slash with
weapons they may have retained from the living days,
or bash opponents with their powerful bony fists.
In addition to bashing and slashing opponents, Lesser
Skeletons drain 1 Constitution bonus per round after 3
rounds to any living being within a 5' radius who fail
a -10 Physical RR.
14.4. skeletons
A skeleton is the most resistant part of the body. Nec-
romancers sometimes strip corpses of their flesh to
produce these abominations. As the skeleton is what
remains the longest of any corpse, so Skeletons prove
to be the most prolific undead around. The strength
of bones coupled with the strength of undeath makes
skeletons dangerous adversaries. Most are Mindless
horrors that pursue the living to gather some more life
force and destroy that of which they are the antithesis.
Some retain a spark of intelligence and wield their
lesser brethren like weapons.
White bones, stripped of flesh and blood, but still in-
tact, move with a darkly born animation. These willful
bones can be from man or beast, but all are equally ter-
rifying in their vile aspect. Frighteningly intelligent,
Lord and Sovereign Skeletons often wear garments
of quality which hang off their bones in mockery of
human dress. Minor and Lesser possess little except
hostility. Retaining expertise at wielding weapons or
claws and tusks, they scamper to denude bones of
their cloaking flesh at every encounter with the living.
These almost Mindless creatures usually serve the de-
sires of a Necromancer or more powerful Undead. An
Ice Skeleton possesses some modicum of sentience,
```
but wanders singly, freezing all that it touches; shrubs
```
in its wake sparkle with frost, and tree branches grow
icicles. Skeletons are of the following Classes: Less-
```
er and Fodder are Class I; Minor is Class II; Ice and
```
```
Joker are Class III; Noble is Class IV; Red Skeleton
```
is Class V while Sovereign is Class VI. Skeletons of
all Classes are less affected by puncture criticals due
to their form. The various types of Skeletons possess
different means of inflicting damage in a battle with
foes. The abilities of each are discussed below.
```
Skeleton, Fodder (I)Skeleton, Fodder (I)
```
```

```

344
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Creature Law
The Ice Skeleton is shrouded by a mist of evaporating
ice. In addition to the 10’ radius of Fear which these
Undead constantly generate, they prefer to bash foes
with their supernaturally enhanced limbs which deliv-
ers an additional Cold critical of equal severity to any
critical normally delivered. Also, it will begin to drain
1 Constitution bonus per round after 3 rounds to any
```
within a 10' radius who fail a Physical RR (with a spe-
```
```
cial modification of -10). Fire attacks are considered
```
one Size larger against these creatures.
The greater talents of the Minor Skeleton in the use
of a weapon and the strength of its limbs are com-
plimented by its stronger ability to drain vitality from
```
the living; after 3 rounds, any living beings within a
```
```
15' radius must make a Physical RR (with a special
```
```
modification of - 10) or be drained of 1 Constitution
```
bonus per round.

345
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Creature Law
Skeleton Nobles have retained their intelligence and
some power from their transformation to Undead
form, and will usually keep a guard of 2-12 Class I-II
Undead with it all times. Wears garments of luxury
and, perhaps, some armor. These Undead possess all
the abilities of a Minor Skeleton and can cast spells.
Dressed in rotting court jester attire, this skeleton
might well have a hump back or be a midget or pos-
sess some other deformity. The pranks and frolics of
this skeleton are not as funny as they might once have
been. Intelligence is buried deep within, and their
lairs are full of traps and deceptions painstakingly ar-
ranged to hurt those trying to enter. At every turn, the
Joker Skeleton will laugh at those caught in its traps
and make fun of them. Some cast spells, some throw
rock pies, others rely on totally defending themselves
in melee, while draining those in the vicinity as they
have the same powers as a Minor Skeleton.

346
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Creature Law
These Undead possess all the abilities of a Noble
Skeleton and, in accordance with their greater power,
the Skeleton Sovereign keeps a larger cadre typically
consisting of 20-200 Class I-II Undead, as well as 1-5
Noble Skeletons, to help it unfold its nefarious plots
for ever greater influence and power. In keeping with
its status, the Skeleton Sovereign drapes luxurious
clothing on its bony form or wears some form of ar-
mor.
This Skeleton has no funny bone in its body, still it
torments its victims with one bad joke after the other.
It throws rotten pies, squirts acid from flowers, presses
battleweed in peoples’ faces and pulls every nefarious
trick and prank in the book. These Skeletons are ex-
tremely intelligent and dangerous. They avoid melee
and instead lure people in traps, make use of their spell
repertoire, engage in a pie throwing fight or execute
well-laid ambushes when victims try to chase them
further in to their lairs.
```
Skeleton, Red (V)Skeleton, Red (V)
```
```
Category: Undead, skeletons
```
```
Archetype: Semi spell user
```
```
Size: 5'9", 25 lbs
```
```
Armor: bone plate AT 6
```
```
Heal: x0.5
```
```
Treasure: -
```

347
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Creature Law
These fluffy, white rabbits seem most innocent. Even
their red eyes don't really stir suspicion, until they hop
up to a person and allow themselves to be petted and
picked up. That is the moment the bunny will strike
with terrible swiftness, taking its razor-sharp teeth
to its victim's throat and jugular vein. They spread
vampirism in their wake, as they drain a victim's life
energy before the victim dies of blood loss, as they
cannot drain their victim's blood completely. Vampire
bunnies make their own minions, but don't control
them like a sentient vampire would, leaving them free
to do what they want. The bunnies have only animal
intelligence and will go after their own kind, but hu-
manoids stir their bloodlust to a high degree. Curse the
vampire that originally thought of making a minion of
a rabbit. They are Class III Undead and share most of
the traits of vampires, except they do not gain the use
of spell lists. They drain 10 Hits per round on the bite
```
attack (-10 Physical RR) and their touch also drains
```
1 Constitution bonus per round, if a Physical RR at
-10 is failed. They share the humanoid vampires’
weaknesses against Water and Lightning and wooden
puncture weapons and they shun the sun, coming out
only at night. They are immune to “m” type spells.
14.5. vampiRes
Vampires are among the nastiest of the undead. In-
stead of appearing as corpses, they retain their living
appearance and they gain a plethora of special of-
fensive and defensive talents. Such power comes at
a price: they need to feed on a living creature’s life
blood every night, or slowly waste away. Also they
have some striking weaknesses that can prove their
undoing against knowledgeable adversaries. Magi-
cal, silver and wood, as well as Lightning and Water
can hurt them. Also, they shun sunlight, for it rapidly
burns them to a cinder.
```
Vampire, Bunny (III)Vampire, Bunny (III)
```

ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Creature Law
during its daily repose with the earth where its body
was interred. However, many of these beings have
learned the trick of transporting the earth overland
when they are in need of moving to a new domicile.
Finally, Vampires cast no reflection in a mirror. Major
are Class V Undead, Lord are Class VI Undead,
and Sovereign are Class VI+. These powerful Un-
dead can call on a host of powers during their noc-
turnal hunts. Physically they are capable of draining
15 hits per round for Major and Lord types and 25 for
Sovereign Vampires, with a successful bite attack
```
(-10 RR versus Physical applicable), along with
```
draining 1 Constitution bonus for Major Vampires,
Lord Vampires drain 2 Constitution bonus and Sov-
ereign 3 Constitution bonus per round for those who
VAMPIRES - HUMAN
In almost every case, male or female, Vampires are
gifted with exceptional presence and appearance.
Their sculpted faces and porcelain skin tones are
home to eyes as deep as the Void and filled with an
all-consuming hunger. This hunger manifests itself
as a driving motivation and an intense passion which
colors every aspect of a Vampire’s unlife. Vampires
prey on the living to sustain their own existence. They
require blood drawn freshly from healthy “donors”
at least once a night, or they begin to degenerate. A
Major or Lord Vampire who has gone without fresh
blood for an entire month will be rendered completely
```
helpless, unable to move (Sovereign Vampires may
```
ignore the final stage and still be active, albeit in a
very reduced state, with the full -100 penalty and the
```
appearance of a shriveled and emaciated centenarian).
```
They can exist in this fashion for centuries, lost in a
hellish blood dream, constantly tormented by an un-
quenchable thirst.
To assist them in their hunt for blood, Vampires usu-
ally maintain some sort of holding or stronghold,
to help protect them from some of their various
weaknesses, which include a susceptibility to di-
```
rect sunlight (sunlight delivers an ‘E’ Heat critical
```
```
to a Vampire for every round they are exposed) and
```
the inability to cross running water under their own
power. Also, a Vampire must make regular contact
```
Vampire, Major (V)Vampire, Major (V)
```

```
fail a Physical RR (with a special modification of -10).
```

to death by a Vampire will rise from the grave as a

ular hold on the minds of others, giving them Mind
```

```

```
are immune to Mental (m type) spells. Due to their
```
strong mystical constitution, these beings can only be
harmed by magical or silver-forged weapons. Puncture
criticals from wooden stakes or similar weapons also
affect these creatures. Elemental Water and Lightning
spells will harm them as opposed to other elements.

ROLEMASTER

Creature LawChapter 15:Chapter 15:
EncountersEncounters
352
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Creature Law
Also, it is best that the GM keeps track of Player Char-
acter total levels. An average encounter of a total num-
ber equal to that of the party with average levels near
those of the party will lead to having a fair encounter.
Increasing the number of combatants in the encounter
will make it harder and decreasing their number, or
lowering their level will make it easier.
```
Example: The GM wants a serious boss fight against
```
the crime lord of the region. He sets the PL at He-
roic and figures his players total levels at: 4 times
9th with a 6th lvl companion and an 8th lvl NPC for
a total of 50. The crime lord is 15th lvl, his 3 body
guards each 7th and they are surrounded by 3rd and
4th lvl thugs, 3 of each, for a total of 57. This is going
to be a slightly tough fight.
It should also be noted that if the players/characters
utilize good tactics, that they may be able to easily
handle an encounter of above equal levels to the char-
acters. If so, then the GM may find it necessary to
sometimes design encounters with a total creature
and/or NPC levels higher than that of the characters.
On the other hand, new players, who do not yet have
very much tactical RPG experience, may struggle with
an encounter intended to be an even fight for them, in
which case the GM may want to drop hints to encour-
age tactical thinking before combat starts.
Monte’s House Rule:
Whenever a 66 is rolled during combat, roll a d10.
If it is also a 6, a tear opens in the fabric of reality
and a random demon appears, normally bent on de-
```
struction (although its attention may be focused on
```
```
people or things other than the PCs). This house-rule
```
was created for a particular campaign, but could be
appropriate for any setting that is dimensionally un-
stable or highly magical. It could also apply only in
specific times and places that have become unstable
due to gates, incursions, or other disturbances. If you
prefer a smaller chance, roll d100 and an additional
66 is required.
Meeting up with any friend, adversary or neutral be-
ing in game is called an encounter. Encounters drive
the action and could be beneficial or dangerous or
anything in between. While beneficial and neutral en-
counters could have any set up the GM deems appro-
priate, it is probably a good idea to balance encounters
with adversaries to avoid making encounters too easy
or too hard for the Player Characters.
It is recommended that the GM detail most of the en-
counters according to the setting and storyline or plots
that the characters are working through.
15.1. B alance
Balancing encounters has always been a tricky busi-
ness. Too many easy encounters make the players
either feel too strong or the world too weak. In both
cases the players start making mistakes which could
lead to the death of party members or loved ones. En-
counters that are systematically too hard leads to frus-
tration and overcaution. Players that are frustrated do
not enjoy themselves and those that take the route of
overcaution will slow down the game with preparation
and deliberation, also not having fun.
Both cases might be a deliberate choice of the GM,
because boss fights should be hard and lowly minion
fights should not be too hard.
A simple adjustment to the difficulty level of an en-
```
counter is to adjust the Power Level (PL) of the foes.
```
If the PL of the foes is lower than that of the PCs,
```
the difficulty of the encounter will be reduced; foes
```
of higher PL will increase the difficulty. For NPCs,
```
see RMCL section 12.1 when creating the characters;
```
the PL will mainly adjust the stats and knack bonuses
of the foes. For creatures, creatures listed in this book
are of the Average PL, but use the modifiers on Table
2.3 to adjust them if a higher power level is desired.
Mix and match PLs in a single encounter to make boss
creatures or runts.
```
Example: The GM is introducing a new group of
```
adversaries in the campaign. He starts off with a
violent encounter between a group of bandits and
the Player Character party. He intends the bandits to
receive a sound beating. Since the players made their
characters using the Heroic PL, he sets the PL of
the encounter to Average. The bandits will therefore
have total stat bonuses around 10 lower than the PCs,
not have any knack bonuses, and will not have access
to talents worth more than 10 DP. Typically, this will
reduce their best skills by around -10 and their DB by
```
-6 (mainly due to lower Quickness).
```
15. Encounters15. Encounters
353
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Creature Law
15.3. misadjudication
GMs are only people and when an encounter seems
to be too harsh, it is always wise for the GM to have
a backup plan. Help could be arriving, or instead of
being slaughtered the PCs are offered quarter. When
```
the encounter seems too light; reinforcements show
```
up or the villain senses the failure early and attempts
to escape. A lot of GM tricks are available and usable
at the last moment.
15.4. encounteR taBles
The chance of a random encounter occurring can be
determined using the following mechanism, which
involves an open-ended d100 “Avoidance Roll” by
one of the players and an open-ended d100 “Activity
Roll” made by the GM. These rolls are then modified
```
as shown in Encounter Modifiers Table (Table 15-4a),
```
```
with the Encounter Differential (the Adjusted Activity
```
```
Roll minus the Adjusted Avoidance Roll) resulting
```
```
in an encounter being avoided (if the differential is
```
```
below 1 (zero or negative), and if there is pursuit, the
```
trail is lost. Whereas if the differential is positive, ref-
```
erence the Standard Encounters Table (Table 15-4b) to
```
determine the type of encounter that occurs as per the
notes at the bottom of the Standard Encounter Table.
15.2. ciRcumstances
The setting of the encounter is going to play a role in
how hard it is. Imagine attacking a gatehouse filled
with the ghosts of soldiers. They will use the defensive
features to their advantage. Also, when a fire demon
is encountered in the magma chamber of a volcano,
it is going to be completely immune to any effects of
the flame, while the Players probably have to dodge
magma gouts and flying lava bombs, not to mention
keeping off the lava. Conditions also might be favor-
able. Summoning that same fire demon on a glacier
will incur automatic damage to the demon. Another is
encountering a group of raving madmen in a meadow
while the whole party is mounted is pretty favorable.
There are several ways of using terrain to steer the
encounter.
ACCESSIBILITY
Putting archers on a ledge or axemen next to a moat is
a good idea. Disrupting a path of advance is going to
work to the PCs’ disadvantage as they cannot pick off
the main targets. Leaders should be surrounded with
```
guards; doorways should be blocked in such a way to
```
allow only one PC to attack more enemies. Of course,
this same doorway could be the choke point for the
PCs’ next attack Or teleporting a strong melee fight-
er onto the archers’ ledge will turn the battle around
easily as the archers are left to fend for themselves,
without help from their melee fighters.
Difficult terrain and slopes and cover can all be used to
try and influence a fight. Also, a way of getting around
the adversary to come at them from two directions is
a good tactic.
DANGEROUS TERRAIN
Terrain that is harmful can be used to restrict access,
but also as a trap to hurt the PCs. Unstable ground,
loose snow and lava pits all have their uses. They can
also be used to hurt the enemy by luring or throwing
them on the dangerous terrain.
AMBUSH
This circumstance happens when the PCs are unsus-
pecting or when they fail to notice an imminent at-
tack. This circumstance is very debilitating and PCs
should strive to avoid such situations but try to create
this situation for themselves. An ambush allows the
ambusher free attacks and to execute their attack plan
to the fullest. Getting caught in an ambush might be
a great challenge to escape doom and turn it around.

355
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Creature Law
If a roll results in a value greater than 300 or less than
01, then it is up to the GM if they want to simply use
the high or low result on the table, or “roll over” to the
top or bottom of the table. As an example, an OE-low
can result in a very high result, indicating that the char-
acters are unlucky enough to encounter an extremely
powerful creature, that they may not be able to defeat
in combat. As such the players/characters should con-
```
sider non-combat options (Hiding, fleeing, etc.).
```
Any Unmodified roll of 66 on the encounter tables,
indicates a Special situation or occurrence, as per the
Rolemaster dice mechanics. It is up to the GM to
determine what this Special situation or encounter is.
The following are also suggestions:
```
Example: A party of 6 (-20 on the Avoidance roll)
```
```
is traveling overland at a walk pace (+0 on the
```
```
Avoidance roll), and avoiding the use of camp-
```
fires. Their Avoidance roll is 32 -20 = 12. They are
```
traveling through mountainous (alpine) terrain
```
```
(+0 on the Activity roll) and the local populace is
```
```
hostile (+25). The GM’s Activity roll is 82 +25 =
```
107. The Activity roll is higher, so an encounter is
```
possible; the difference is 107 – 12 = 95. The region
```
is only lightly inhabited, so this gives a result of
```
basic/sighted (“b / s”). The GM uses the Alpine
```
encounter table, rolling 58, resulting in a Big Ogre.
The GM decides a close encounter with an Ogre
is unlikely to be neutral, so the PCs sight the Ogre
at a distance. Now as long as they can keep from
being noticed by the Ogre, they may be able to get
away without a fight. Ogres do not have too sensi-
tive a sense of smell, but they do have keen hearing,
so hopefully the PCs can avoid making too much
noise!
The random encounter tables presented on the follow-
ing pages include all the creatures detailed within this
work. Use them when a random encounter is appropri-
```
ate rather than a specific encounter (e.g., if the PCs are
```
evading enemy forces, then an unlucky avoidance roll
```
may result in an encounter with those specific forces).
```
```
Tables appear for each Biome as listed in RMCL (with
```
```
the Biome code designated in the title), as well as for
```
```
special locations such as Cities/Civilized (C), Grange/
```
```
farmlands/grazelands (G), Enchanted/Extraplanar (E)
```
```
locations, Haunted (H) (including burial grounds or
```
```
former battlefields), Ruins (R) and Volcanic (V) loca-
```
tions. These are also the locations which appear within
the Biome locations section of the creature tables indi-
cating where the creatures are commonly found. You
may notice that some creatures may not appear in the
table specific to a Biome that is designated or that they
may appear on a table that is not designated within
their preferred Biome listing in the table. In these cas-
es, it is usually due to the creatures being listed within
another table that is referenced within the Biome Ta-
ble, with an increased chance of being rolled.
Most of the tables range from 1 to 300, using
Open-Ended High rolls, however the Universal An-
```
imal and Grange/Grazeland tables are 01-100 (not
```
```
open ended) as noted in the heading of these two ta-
```
bles. In general, higher rolls result in more powerful
encounters. Occasionally results will indicate terrain
features or other challenges rather than creatures at all,
```
especially in biomes where animals are sparse (e.g.,
```
```
Ice Caps & Polar Desert).
```
While this is intended as a guide for random encoun-
ters, the GM should adjust the encounter by adding
additional combatants to the encounter if needed to
create an appropriate challenge. Ideally, the total levels
of the creatures or NPC’s should be roughly balanced
with all of the characters that will be participating in
the encounter, for an encounter of a typical difficulty.
Of course, if you want the encounter to be relatively
easy, reduce the total number of levels of the crea-
tures. Occasional easy encounters let the players see
that their characters have advanced in power, while
```
occasional near-impossible encounters (which they
```
```
may need to flee from) remind them that the world can
```
be dangerous.
Any time the result of a table references a universal
```
table (Univ. Animal, Univ. Monster, or Univ. Race),
```
then roll on the designated Universal table for the type
of creature, with any modifier noted.
```
Whenever an entry has a super-script W or L (small
```
letter raised at the end of the name, example: “Fish,
mediumW” indicating that it only occurs when Water is
```
present within the Biome (river, lake, swamp, etc.), or
```
```
where Land is present (a small island or coral atoll) on
```
the Ocean Biome table. Also note that if the Univer-
sal Race is rolled on the Ocean Biome table, the GM
should assume that they are encountered on a boat or
ship, or maybe some sort of wreckage.
Table 15.4c: Special situation or
encounter suggestions

extra-dimensional portal, magical runic

enchanted spring

373
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED
For over four decades, Rolemaster has been the definitive fantasyFor over four decades, Rolemaster has been the definitive fantasy
role-playing game that combines realism and depth withoutrole-playing game that combines realism and depth without
sacrificing playability. Rolemaster Unified is the next evolutionsacrificing playability. Rolemaster Unified is the next evolution
drawing upon the best elements of previous editions to createdrawing upon the best elements of previous editions to create
a streamlined and consistent game.a streamlined and consistent game. Rolemaster Creature LawRolemaster Creature Law
II provides the creatures and monsters that players will encounterprovides the creatures and monsters that players will encounter
and oppose.and oppose.
This first volume ofThis first volume of Creature LawCreature Law offers well over 400 creatures,offers well over 400 creatures,
created with the same fundamentals as characters, but stream-created with the same fundamentals as characters, but stream-
lined with the use of archetypes.lined with the use of archetypes. Creature Law ICreature Law I details for eachdetails for each
their abilities, skills, and stats in the same terms as characters,their abilities, skills, and stats in the same terms as characters,
with the addition of 225 new talents and flaws reflecting creaturewith the addition of 225 new talents and flaws reflecting creature
abilities and weaknesses, including fiery breath, petrifying gazeabilities and weaknesses, including fiery breath, petrifying gaze
attacks, acid coatings, ink glands, extra limbs, shapechanging,attacks, acid coatings, ink glands, extra limbs, shapechanging,
echolocation, presence senses and many more.echolocation, presence senses and many more.
Imperil the player-characters with the very real threats of tigersImperil the player-characters with the very real threats of tigers
and sharks, or surprise them with dinosaurs believed extinctand sharks, or surprise them with dinosaurs believed extinct
or unearthly animals such as the jackalope. These dangers paleor unearthly animals such as the jackalope. These dangers pale
against the greater capabilities of griffins, minotaurs, demonagainst the greater capabilities of griffins, minotaurs, demon
whales, unicorns and other legendary monsters. Encounters withwhales, unicorns and other legendary monsters. Encounters with
dragons and their lesser fell kin of wyverns, lesser drakes, hydrasdragons and their lesser fell kin of wyverns, lesser drakes, hydras
and basilisks will shape epic sagas. A variety of animated, poi-and basilisks will shape epic sagas. A variety of animated, poi-
sonous and entrapping plants will pose other sorts of challengessonous and entrapping plants will pose other sorts of challenges
for even the most experienced adventurer. Artificial creations,for even the most experienced adventurer. Artificial creations,
whether construct, golem, gargoyle or the incredibly fast Shards,whether construct, golem, gargoyle or the incredibly fast Shards,
may prove implacable foes. Elementals and elementally infusedmay prove implacable foes. Elementals and elementally infused
creatures such as the destructive zephyr hounds claim the heartcreatures such as the destructive zephyr hounds claim the heart
of the wilds as their own. Heroes may pit their blades and magicof the wilds as their own. Heroes may pit their blades and magic
against djinn and demons from other planes. Fey from the dimin-against djinn and demons from other planes. Fey from the dimin-
utive pysks and sylphs to pookas and undines can be uncannyutive pysks and sylphs to pookas and undines can be uncanny
opponents, while garks, giants, centaurs and many others mayopponents, while garks, giants, centaurs and many others may
be deadly rivals. And from beyond the grave, the unquiet dead -be deadly rivals. And from beyond the grave, the unquiet dead -
skeletons, ghosts, ghouls, wraiths and vampires – seek sustenanceskeletons, ghosts, ghouls, wraiths and vampires – seek sustenance
and vengeance from the living.and vengeance from the living.
Finally for the gamemaster who wants to let the dice suggest theFinally for the gamemaster who wants to let the dice suggest the
next challenge,next challenge, Creature Law ICreature Law I offers encounter tables for eachoffers encounter tables for each
biome, plus encounters for underground, urban, and farmlandbiome, plus encounters for underground, urban, and farmland
locations, and easy-to-use indexes of creatures, talents, andlocations, and easy-to-use indexes of creatures, talents, and
